evals.expected_output: The skill should flag all three tables as missing RLS and provide corrected migrations with RLS enabled and appropriate policies for each table. DMs should only be readable by sender and recipient.
assertions: Flags missing Row Level Security on all three tables as CRITICAL
Adds ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY for each table
Creates RLS policies that use auth.uid() for access control
DM policies restrict access to sender_id and recipient_id only
Posts table has a public-read or authenticated-read policy
Mentions the Moltbook breach or a real-world example of why RLS matters

evals.expected_output: The skill should flag the hardcoded OpenAI API key in client-side code as CRITICAL, explain it's visible to anyone in the browser, and provide a fix that moves the API call to a server-side API route.
assertions: Flags the hardcoded OpenAI API key as a CRITICAL vulnerability
Explains the key is visible in the browser/client bundle
Provides a server-side API route (e.g., app/api/chat/route.ts) as the fix
The fix uses process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY without a NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix
Does NOT just say 'use an environment variable' in the client component — moves the call server-side

evals.prompt: Can you write a Supabase Edge Function that lets users store their API keys for different AI services? I want them to be able to save their OpenAI key, Anthropic key, etc. and then use them through my platform.
evals.expected_output: The skill should warn about storing third-party API keys, recommend encryption if storage is necessary, and ensure the edge function validates authentication. Should suggest alternatives to storing keys.
assertions: Warns about the risks of storing third-party API keys (references cascading breach risk)
Suggests alternatives to storing keys (OAuth, proxy with platform key, in-memory only)
If it does store keys, encrypts them at rest (not plaintext)
The edge function requires JWT authentication (verify_jwt: true or manual auth check)
RLS policies ensure users can only access their own stored keys
Mentions that plaintext key storage is what made the Moltbook breach cascade to other services
